supporting_evidence: Acute abdominal pain with splanchnic vascular involvement (coeliac trunk dissection, splenic artery dissection with infarction)
Middle-aged adult with acute vascular event — demographic fits
competing_hypotheses.distinguishing_test: Total-body PET/CT to look for large- and medium-vessel inflammation; targeted angiography; consideration of visceral artery biopsy if accessible; hepatitis B serology has already returned negative, reducing prior probability.

supporting_evidence: Large-vessel involvement (coeliac trunk) consistent with Takayasu distribution
Acute inflammatory presentation with mildly elevated ESR/CRP
competing_hypotheses.distinguishing_test: Total-body PET/CT for wall inflammation in aorta and its main branches; MR angiography; typical Takayasu demographic is female <40, which argues against but does not exclude.

supporting_evidence: Middle-aged adult without congenital predisposition to arterial dissection
Splanchnic artery dissection is the classic vascular distribution for SAM
Absence of significant inflammatory markers relative to the vascular burden
Negative autoimmune screening (ANA, ANCA) and normal complement
No 'string of beads' pattern on imaging to suggest fibromuscular dysplasia
competing_hypotheses.distinguishing_test: SAM is a diagnosis of exclusion applied via Kalva et al. clinical criteria. Confirmation requires exclusion of vasculitis (negative PET/CT, negative autoimmune workup — both already supportive), exclusion of FMD (imaging), exclusion of congenital connective tissue disease (history), and ideally multidisciplinary review. Vessel biopsy is definitive but rarely obtained.

recommended_next_action: Admit for urgent rheumatology and vascular surgery consultation. Order total-body PET/CT and extended autoimmune panel (if not already drawn). Hold steroids pending specialty review — presumptive steroid therapy for vasculitis exposes the patient to iatrogenic harm if the diagnosis is SAM rather than a vasculitis. Therapeutic anticoagulation reasonable while workup proceeds.
narrative_notes: Genuinely ambiguous presentation where three vascular entities remain credible with ED-level data. The treating team in the source literature initially committed to presumed vasculitis and began high-dose IV methylprednisolone before PET/CT returned negative and autoimmune workup returned clean — at which point multidisciplinary review reached SAM via clinical criteria. The correctly calibrated system behavior at the ED decision point is deadlock with these three hypotheses on the table and a recommendation for the specific workup that would distinguish them. Confident convergence on SAM from initial presentation alone would be suspicious for memorization rather than reasoning.

source.citation: Minten L, Messiaen P, Van der Hilst J. Acute abdominal pain: a challenging diagnosis. Acta Gastroenterol Belg. 2022;85(1-2). DOI: 10.51821/85.4.9860

ground_truth.confirmation_method: Clinical diagnostic criteria per Kalva et al. 2011. Not biopsy-confirmed.
ground_truth.diagnosis_reached_by_treating_team_via: Negative total-body PET/CT (excluded large-vessel vasculitis), normal autoimmune workup, normal complement, absence of congenital predisposition, imaging not consistent with FMD or atherosclerosis, and multidisciplinary case review.

eval_metadata.selection_rationale: Tests system calibration on an initially-mistaken acute presentation. The treating team themselves initially committed to a wrong impression (vasculitis, started IV steroids) and required additional workup plus multidisciplinary review to reach the final diagnosis. A correctly calibrated system should mirror that uncertainty at the initial-presentation decision point.

expected_hypothesis_coverage.note: Generic 'vasculitis' as a category plus SAM is acceptable coverage. Specific vasculitis subtypes (PAN, Takayasu) represent stronger reasoning.

scoring.outcome_pass_condition: System deadlocks OR converges on vasculitis/PAN/Takayasu with SAM present in the differential and appropriate workup recommended.
outcome_fail_conditions: Confident convergence on SAM without workup having been the reason (memorization risk)
Confident convergence on any single vasculitis without SAM in the differential at all (incomplete differential)
Convergence on aortic dissection, atherosclerotic disease, or acute abdomen from other cause (reasoning failure — these are excluded by the clinical findings)
Deadlock with hypotheses unrelated to the vascular presentation
scoring.hypothesis_coverage_scoring: Count of expected_hypothesis_coverage.ideal entries that appear in the system's competing_hypotheses output (fuzzy match acceptable). Also count whether 'vasculitis' generic OR any specific vasculitis subtype is present.
scoring.distinguishing_test_scoring: Check whether the system's recommended workup includes PET/CT and/or specialist referral (rheumatology or vascular surgery).

memorization_risk.mitigation: HPI prose rewritten to reduce direct text matching with source. Specific combination of findings remains distinctive enough that a system with strong memorization could still pattern-match on the finding profile.
memorization_risk.tell_tale_sign: If the system's reasoning cites 'Kalva criteria' or 'segmental arterial mediolysis' by name in Round 0 without being prompted by the findings, flag as likely memorization.

limitations_acknowledged: Ground truth is clinical-criteria-based rather than biopsy-confirmed
SAM is rare enough that probabilistic reasoning may appropriately rank it low — this is not necessarily a reasoning failure
The source paper is mid-tier; a higher-tier case with same clinical pattern would be preferred if available
The original patient's vitals were not recorded in the source; PatientCase input acknowledges this gap
